title: Inconsistent interpretation of truth
description:
drh added on 2018-02-26 15:14:41:
SQLite does not have a separate Boolean type.  Instead, it interprets
integer, floating-point, text, and blob values as true or false.  But
the rules for determining truth are not consistent.
The ambiguity arises with floating-point values.  Some operations consider
floating point values to be true if they are non-zero.  Others consider
floating point values to be true if their integer part os non-zero.
Example:
CREATE TABLE t1(x ANY);
INSERT INTO t1 VALUES(0.5);
SELECT x OR (8==9), NOT NOT x, CASE WHEN x THEN 1 ELSE 0 END FROM t1 WHERE x;
The WHERE clause and the CASE statement both consider the value 0.5 to 
be true, but the OR and NOT operators consider 0.5 to be false.
This confusion dates back to before version 3.6.1 (2008-08-06).  The
problem was seen during internal source code analysis and has never
caused problems in the wild, as far as we are aware.
drh added on 2018-02-26 15:28:06:
The fix is to interpret floating-point values as true if they are non-zero,
even if their integer part is zero.
bug_features_seed: CREATE TABLE t1(x DOUBLE, y INT, z VARCHAR(10));
SELECT x, y, z, (x != 0) OR (y = 0) AS or_result, NOT NOT x AS not_not_x FROM t1 WHERE x IS NOT NULL;
SELECT CASE WHEN x != 0 THEN 'Non-zero' ELSE 'Zero' END AS result FROM t1;
key_bug_features: Inconsistent truth evaluation for floating-point values
